chunk_summary: This section discusses strategies to prevent cholestasis and monitor metabolic bone disease in patients on prolonged parenteral nutrition, focusing on risk factors, prevention, and monitoring protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand the risk factors and prevention strategies for cholestasis in patients on prolonged parenteral nutrition.
Learn the importance of early enteral feeding and cycling PN to reduce hepatobiliary complications.
Identify monitoring protocols for liver enzymes, conjugated bilirubin, and bone health markers in PN-dependent patients.
Recognize the role of lipid emulsions and nutrient adjustments in managing intestinal failure–associated liver disease (IFALD).
key_facts: Cholestasis risk increases with prematurity, sepsis, prolonged lack of enteral feeding, and overfeeding (especially excess dextrose and lipid).
Cycling PN (e.g., 12–18 hours/day) can provide a hepatic rest period and reduce cholestasis risk.
Mixed-oil or fish-oil lipid emulsions are preferred for prolonged intestinal failure when available.
Monitor liver enzymes and conjugated bilirubin weekly in children on ongoing PN.
Metabolic bone disease risk is highest with prolonged PN, inadequate calcium/phosphate delivery, chronic inflammation, steroid use, and poor vitamin D status.
Periodic monitoring of calcium, phosphate, magnesium, and alkaline phosphatase is essential to detect metabolic bone disease.
reference_quote: Cholestasis risk rises with prematurity, sepsis, prolonged lack of enteral stimulation, and iatrogenic overfeeding (especially excess dextrose and lipid).
document_key_topics: Monitoring protocols for liver and bone health
Role of lipid emulsions in IFALD management
Risk factors for metabolic bone disease
